title: Quiz: Quantum Walks on Graphs
description: Test your understanding of discrete-time and continuous-time quantum walks and their applications

questions.question: In a continuous-time quantum walk on a graph with adjacency matrix A, the evolution operator is:
questions.explanation: The continuous-time quantum walk follows Schrodinger equation dynamics with the adjacency matrix as the Hamiltonian: |psi(t)> = e^{-iAt}|psi(0)>. The -i factor ensures unitary evolution since A is Hermitian (symmetric for real graphs).

questions.question: Why does a discrete-time quantum walk require a coin register in addition to the position register?

options.text: To enable measurement without disturbing the walk
To make the evolution unitary when the graph has varying degrees

questions.explanation: Without the coin, a direct translation of the classical random walk transition matrix would not be unitary. The coin provides the additional degree of freedom needed to construct a unitary evolution operator. The coin operation and shift operation together form the unitary walk step.

questions.question: How does the variance of position in a quantum walk compare to a classical random walk after t steps?
questions.explanation: Classical random walks exhibit diffusive spreading with variance growing as O(t). Quantum walks show ballistic spreading with variance growing as O(t^2). This quadratic speedup in spreading is the foundation for quantum walk algorithmic advantages.

questions.question: For the quantum walk search algorithm on a complete graph with n vertices, what is the expected number of steps to find a marked vertex?
questions.explanation: The quantum walk search algorithm on a complete graph achieves optimal O(sqrt(n)) complexity, matching Grover's search. This is a quadratic speedup over the classical O(n) search time. The high connectivity of the complete graph enables this optimal speedup.

questions.question: The Hadamard coin in a 1D discrete-time quantum walk produces:

options.text: Uniform probability distribution at all times
Asymmetric probability distribution biased toward one direction
Symmetric distribution only for symmetric initial coin states

questions.explanation: The Hadamard walk produces a symmetric probability distribution only when the initial coin state is symmetric (e.g., |0> + i|1>)/sqrt(2)). Starting with |0> or |1> produces a characteristic asymmetric distribution due to the phases introduced by the Hadamard operation.

questions.question: In a continuous-time quantum walk, the graph Laplacian L = D - A can be used as the Hamiltonian instead of the adjacency matrix A.
questions.explanation: True. Both the adjacency matrix A and the graph Laplacian L = D - A (where D is the degree matrix) can serve as valid Hamiltonians for continuous-time quantum walks. They produce different dynamics but both lead to meaningful quantum walk behavior. The Laplacian is often preferred as it connects to spectral graph theory.

questions.question: What is the Grover diffusion operator used for in coined quantum walks on general graphs?

options.text: It serves as the shift operator
It is applied as the coin operation at each vertex
It marks the target vertex for search

questions.explanation: The Grover diffusion operator (2|s><s| - I, where |s> is the uniform superposition) is commonly used as the coin operator at each vertex in coined walks on general graphs. It provides a natural generalization of the Hadamard coin for vertices with degree greater than 2.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about the relationship between quantum walks and quantum speedup? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The spectral gap of the graph affects the achievable speedup
All graphs provide the same O(sqrt(n)) speedup for search
Quantum interference is essential for the speedup
Element distinctness achieves O(n^{2/3}) via quantum walks

questions.explanation: The spectral gap determines mixing and hitting times (a). Quantum interference enables probability amplitude to accumulate at target states (c). Element distinctness achieves O(n^{2/3}) query complexity using quantum walks (d). However, the speedup varies by graph structure - 2D grids give O(sqrt(n log n)) rather than optimal O(sqrt(n)) (b is false).
